region_of_origin: Global (widely produced in Europe, USA, Australia, China)

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for canned pear in syrup; SNOMED CT coverage of processed fruit products is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 120 g, equivalent to one half of a canned pear with a small amount of accompanying syrup.

nutrient_source.title: Canned pear, in syrup — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for canned pear in syrup (cr_id F04030013). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this record at confidence 0.95. Saturated fat, monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and micronutrients not reported by HPB are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC; canned fruit in syrup analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry for canned pear in heavy syrup was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 44 is derived from the closest analogous entry: 'Pear, canned in natural juice' (GI ~44, University of Sydney GI Database). Canned pear in heavy syrup is expected to have a similar or slightly higher GI due to added sucrose; however, the fibre content (1.6 g/100g) moderates the glycaemic response. GI 44 is used as a conservative lower-bound estimate. Glycaemic load per 120 g serving = (44 × 19.17 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 10.1 g.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar (15.2 g/100g) with low dietary fibre (1.6 g/100g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention despite very low sodium content (5 mg/100g)
hypertension.guidance: Although sodium is negligible at 5 mg/100g, the high sugar load from heavy syrup (15.2 g/100g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention; individuals with hypertension should choose pears canned in water or natural juice and limit portion size to 60–80 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Sugar 15.2 g/100g with dietary fibre only 1.6 g/100g; estimated GI 44 but glycaemic load per standard serving (~10 g) is clinically significant for blood glucose management
type2_diabetes.guidance: Canned pear in heavy syrup delivers 15.2 g sugar per 100 g with insufficient fibre buffering (1.6 g/100g), producing a meaningful glycaemic load of approximately 10 g per 120 g serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should drain the syrup thoroughly, limit portions to 60 g, and pair with a protein source to blunt the postprandial glucose rise — fresh pear (GI ~38, fibre ~3.1 g/100g) is a substantially superior alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar from heavy syrup (15.2 g/100g) contributes to elevated triglycerides and hepatic de novo lipogenesis risk; sodium is low (5 mg/100g) and fat is negligible (0.13 g/100g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The very low fat (0.13 g/100g) and sodium (5 mg/100g) profile is favourable for cardiovascular disease, but the high added sugar content (15.2 g/100g) from heavy syrup raises triglyceride risk with regular consumption; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit intake to occasional small portions and prefer pears canned in water or natural juice.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Added sugar at 15.2 g/100g promotes pro-inflammatory cytokine activity; no meaningful anti-inflammatory nutrients present at effective levels
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The high added sugar content (15.2 g/100g) from heavy syrup promotes systemic inflammation relevant to rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with RA should avoid syrup-packed canned fruits as a regular dietary pattern and choose fresh pears or pears canned in water to obtain fibre and polyphenol benefits without the pro-inflammatory sugar load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Canned pear in syrup is a commercially processed fruit product consisting of peeled and halved or sliced pear fruit preserved in a heavy sugar syrup solution, typically containing water and sucrose or high-fructose corn syrup. The product has a pale golden to ivory colour, a soft and tender texture, and a sweet flavour profile that is noticeably sweeter than fresh pear due to the added syrup. The canning process involves heat sterilisation, which softens the fruit and reduces some heat-labile vitamins such as vitamin C compared to fresh pear. Per 100 g, the product provides 74 kcal, 19.17 g carbohydrate (of which 15.2 g is sugar), 0.2 g protein, 0.13 g fat, 1.6 g dietary fibre, and only 5 mg sodium. The high sugar content from the syrup is the primary nutritional concern: it significantly elevates the glycaemic load above that of fresh pear and is associated with increased risk of postprandial glucose spikes, insulin-mediated sodium retention, and elevated triglycerides with regular consumption. The estimated glycaemic index is approximately 44 (low category), but the glycaemic load per standard 120 g serving is approximately 10 g, which is clinically meaningful for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease. Canned pear in syrup is widely available globally and is commonly consumed as a snack, dessert component, or ingredient in fruit salads. It is plant-based, gluten-free, dairy-free, halal, and kosher. For health-conscious consumers, draining the syrup before consumption can reduce sugar intake by approximately 30–40%, and choosing pears canned in natural juice or water is a substantially healthier alternative.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F04030013) at confidence 0.95. The source is an approved primary regional source for Singapore food records. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest defensible match (canned pear in natural juice, GI 44); this introduces minor uncertainty but does not reduce overall confidence to medium given the strong macro provenance.
notes: Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for canned pear in syrup is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database (F04030013) as of 2026-04-16. Vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: these micronutrient and moisture values are not reported in the HPB source for this record. Cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB source; this is a plant-based product and contains no cholesterol by nature. Protein_g (0.2 g) and fat_g (0.13 g) are non-zero and sourced from HPB. No zero-macro justification is required for protein or fat as both are non-zero. GI of 44 is an analogous estimate based on 'Pear, canned in natural juice' from the University of Sydney GI Database; heavy syrup may marginally increase GI above this estimate. Glycaemic load calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (44 × 19.17 × 1.2) / 100 ≈ 10.1 g for a 120 g serving. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; therefore no mandatory local-language names are required under G6 multilingual rules. The food is a globally generic processed fruit product.
